did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning methods, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combined with YOLO and Fast R-CNN for feature extraction and object detection, along with Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) analysis and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) for classification and risk assessment, to detect melanoma and assess risk using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel AI-based melanoma detection system using CNNs, YOLO, Fast R-CNN, and MST analysis. It details a specific methodology, utilizes the HAM10000 dataset for training and validation, and reports original performance results (e.g., AUC of 0.94). Although published in conference proceedings (ICISS-2025), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: AI-based Melanoma Object Detection: Real-Time Risk Assessment using MST
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS-2025)
brief_summary: This study developed an AI-based melanoma detection system for real-time risk assessment by integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), YOLO, and Faster R-CNN with Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) analysis. The model was trained and validated on the HAM10000 dataset, utilizing preprocessing and data augmentation techniques to enhance feature extraction and lesion segmentation. The system combines deep learning for object identification with MST for spatial and textural feature analysis to classify lesions as benign or malignant. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC metrics. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 92.30% and an AUC of 0.94, demonstrating superior performance compared to conventional CNN and YOLO-based approaches. The results suggest that this integrated approach effectively reduces false positives and supports rapid, accurate clinical diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study; the system classifies lesions as melanoma (malignant) or benign. The dataset includes a 'diagnostic type' field distinguishing between melanoma and benign cases.
The negative class in the binary classification task, distinguished from melanoma. The dataset includes a 'diagnostic type' field for benign cases.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses YOLO and Faster R-CNN for real-time object detection and precise lesion localization. Figure 6 shows 'Melanoma Detection using Bounding Box'.

The system classifies lesions as benign or malignant using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier after feature extraction. The primary evaluation metrics include accuracy (92.30%), precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC (0.94) for distinguishing between benign and malignant tumours.

The model calculates a melanoma risk score for each lesion by combining MST values with environmental exposure factors (sun, chemical, bacteria exposure) to categorize lesions into risk categories (low, medium, high). Figure 4 shows 'Melanoma Risk Score Analysis'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesions used for training and validation. The dataset contains parameters for each lesion including identifier, image identifier, diagnostic type (melanoma or benign), age, sex, location, and exposure to sun, chemicals, and bacteria.

use.training: The model was trained using the HAM10000 dataset.
use.validation: The model was validated using the HAM10000 dataset with preprocessing and data augmentation techniques.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification quality and calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC-AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic - Area Under the Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to differentiate between benign and malignant tumours and demonstrate classification performance.
To determine model efficiency by optimizing batch size (32) and learning rate (0.001).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed method combines CNN for feature extraction, Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) for spatial/textural feature analysis, and an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for classification. It incorporates environmental risk factors (sun, chemical, bacteria exposure) to calculate a melanoma risk score. The results demonstrate superior performance compared to conventional CNN and YOLO-based approaches. Hyperparameter tuning used a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of 0.001.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid system combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), YOLO (You Only Look Once), Faster R-CNN (referred to as rapid R-CNN in text), Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) analysis, and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier.

architecture_details.cnn_structure: Three convolutional layers followed by fully connected layers.
architecture_details.object_detection_components: YOLO and Faster R-CNN used for real-time object detection and precise lesion localization.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: CNN extracts features; MST constructs a graph where nodes represent Regions of Interest (ROIs) and edges represent similarity, minimizing edge weight to cluster similar lesions.
architecture_details.framework: TensorFlow/Keras for CNN and YOLO/Faster R-CNN models; OpenCV for preprocessing; scikit-learn for ANN and MST; Matplotlib for visualization.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (hyperparameter tuning was used to determine batch size and learning rate).

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and risk scoring (low, medium, high) based on MST values and environmental exposure factors (sun, chemical, bacteria exposure).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Faster R-CNN (referred to as rapid R-CNN)
preprocessing_techniques: Standardization of environmental risk factor data (sun exposure, chemical exposure, bacteria exposure)
Data augmentation including flipping, rotating, and zooming

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used during validation to increase the model's capacity to generalize to novel and untested data sets.
Used during validation to increase the model's capacity to generalize to novel and untested data sets.

conclusions: The proposed AI-based melanoma detection system, which integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), YOLO, Faster R-CNN, and Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) analysis, achieved an accuracy of 92.30% and an AUC of 0.94 on the HAM10000 dataset.

The integration of MST for spatial and textural feature extraction, combined with environmental risk factors (sun, chemical, and bacteria exposure), enhances the model's ability to distinguish between benign and malignant tumors compared to conventional CNN and YOLO-based approaches.
The system effectively reduces false positives and supports rapid, accurate clinical diagnosis by providing real-time risk assessment and precise lesion localization.

The study acknowledges limitations including potential biases from self-reported environmental characteristics in the simulated dataset and the high computational costs of the MST approach for large datasets.

Future work is directed toward improving computing speed, conducting clinical testing, extending the dataset for better generalization, and incorporating real-time sensor data for environmental risk indicators.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the dataset used is not derived from real-life melanoma cases, introducing potential population or dataset bias.
The model relies on self-reported environmental characteristics (sun, chemical, bacteria exposure) in a simulated dataset, which induces biases and diminishes model usefulness.
The computational costs of the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) approach are noted as a limitation, potentially resulting in longer processing times for large datasets.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance is evaluated using descriptive metrics only.
No external validation was performed; the model was trained and validated solely on the HAM10000 dataset.
The paper does not report the specific loss function or optimizer used, only that hyperparameter tuning determined a batch size of 32 and learning rate of 0.001.
The study is published in conference proceedings (ICISS-2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may affect the rigor of the peer review process.
The input image size is very small (100x100 pixels), which may limit the resolution and detail available for feature extraction compared to standard dermatological imaging.
